items.q: Why does oceanic crust subduct beneath continental crust?
items.correct: Oceanic crust is denser (basaltic) than continental crust (granitic), so when they collide the denser oceanic plate sinks into the mantle, forming a trench and volcanic arc.
items.misconception: Oceanic crust subducts because the ocean pushes it down with its weight.
items.random_wrong: Continental crust subducts because it is thicker and heavier.
items.why: Density, not water pressure, drives subduction. Continental crust is too buoyant to subduct, which is why continent-continent collisions produce fold mountains instead of trenches.

items.q: How do humans affect the carbon cycle?
items.correct: By burning fossil fuels (releasing stored geological carbon to the atmosphere) and deforestation (reducing the biosphere store and releasing carbon).
items.misconception: Humans only affect the carbon cycle by breathing out CO2.
items.random_wrong: Humans affect the carbon cycle by eating plants.
items.why: Human respiration is balanced (we eat carbon that plants captured). The disruption is shifting carbon from long-term geological stores into the active atmospheric cycle.

items.q: What is the difference between primary and secondary data in geography fieldwork?
items.correct: Primary data is collected first-hand by the researcher (surveys, measurements, observations); secondary data already exists and is found in sources (census data, maps, published reports).
items.misconception: Primary data is more important and secondary data is less reliable.
items.random_wrong: Primary data is collected in primary schools and secondary data in secondary schools.
items.why: It's about source, not quality. Secondary data (e.g. Census) is often more reliable than small primary surveys. Good fieldwork triangulates both — the distinction is who collected it, not how useful it is.

items.correct: The surface point above the focus
items.misconception: The deepest point in the ocean
items.why: Correct answer: The surface point above the focus. “The deepest point in the ocean” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The answer examiners want is: Ground shaking. “Rainfall” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many times stronger is a magnitude 7 than magnitude 6 (roughly)?
items.why: The real figure is 10. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
